text: A thoracoscope is inserted through a planned chest tube incision, providing a video recording view of the thoracic cavity.
The patient's spine is positioned towards the left side of the screen, while the lung is retracted anteriorly towards the right side of the screen, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The gastric conduit is prominently visible in the foreground, with the staple line along the lesser curvature oriented laterally.
In the abdominal cavity, a uniform diameter gastric conduit is created using a 100mm stapler with a green cartridge, delivering two rows of staples with a 4.8mm staple height.
The surgeon completes the construction of the gastric conduit using one or two additional fires of a sixty millimeter endoscopic tri stapler, adding rows of staples to secure the conduit structure.
The conduit tip is visible, and no tri staplers are being used on it.
The gastric conduit is visible within the chest cavity, positioned after being delivered from the abdomen.
The esophagus is mobilized up to the level of the gastric conduit tip, ensuring minimal devascularization of the esophageal tissue.

The surgeon begins the esophagogastric anastomosis by aligning the mid-left lateral aspect of the upper third of the intrathoracic esophagus over the lesser curve staple line. Four initial tacking sutures are placed approximately three centimeters apart to secure the alignment.

The surgeon prepares to perform a shorter side-to-side anastomosis to ensure an adequate proximal esophageal margin, adjusting the alignment of the esophagus and gastric conduit within the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon partially transects the esophagus one centimeter distal to the inferior set of tacking sutures, using precise cutting techniques to maintain the integrity of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon completely transects the esophagus in cases of malignancy, removing a ring of esophageal tissue from the surgical specimen for frozen section pathologic examination.
The surgeon creates a two-centimeter gastrotomy in the gastric conduit across the lesser curve staple line, preparing for the esophagogastric anastomosis.
The surgeon places five tacking sutures between the adjacent conduit and esophageal walls, securing the alignment for the esophagogastric anastomosis.
The surgeon places the first suture in the middle of the conduit through the lesser curve staple line.
The surgeon places two sutures on either side of the partially transected esophagus, securing the tissue in preparation for further surgical steps.
The surgeon uses an endoscopic stapler to perform the stapled portion of the anastomosis, completing approximately two-thirds of the total anastomotic diameter.
The surgeon uses a linear, forty-five millimeter green cartridge stapler with a staple height of four point one millimeters, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon places the narrow anvil within the esophageal lumen and positions the large reload jaw within the conduit lumen, preparing for anastomosis.
The surgeon fires the stapler, restapling and cutting through the previously established lesser curve staple line, visibly reinforcing the anastomosis.
The surgeon inspects the side-to-side communication of the anastomosis to ensure complete cutting and stapling has been achieved, visually confirming the integrity of the staple line.
The surgeon completes the esophageal transection using sharp dissection, visibly separating the esophagus from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon places a silk suture at the center of the esophageal opening, using it for retraction to facilitate further surgical manipulation.
The cut end of the esophagus is visibly longer than the gastrotomy, highlighting the difference in lengths between these two structures.
The surgeon removes an elliptical section of the conduit over the lesser curve staple line, avoiding lateral extension of the gastrotomy to preserve the collateral blood supply to the conduit.
The surgeon closes the open common lumen using two layers of sutures. The inner layer consists of inverted, interrupted 3-0 polyglyctin sutures, which are tied after all sutures have been placed.
The surgeon places a second layer of interrupted 3-0 silk sutures using a limbered technique, starting at both corners of the surgical site.
The surgeon places a modified horizontal mattress suture at the junction of the gastric conduit staple line, securing the tissue at this critical point.
The surgeon cuts the initial orienting sutures and inspects the completed anastomosis, checking for proper alignment and integrity of the sutured connection.

A pleural flap is mobilized from the superior aspect over the upper third of the intrathoracic esophagus, providing soft tissue coverage. The flap is secured to the gastric conduit using interrupted three-oh silk sutures.
The surgeon eliminates any residual redundancy in the gastric conduit by closing the open common lumen with sutures and tacking the pleura to the conduit.
The surgeon over-sews the superior aspect of the lesser curve conduit staple line using a limbered technique, extending the suturing inferiorly until reaching the right gastric vessels.
An anesthesia team member passes a nasogastric tube into the conduit, reaching the level of the cruce before the chest is closed.
The surgeon places chest tubes into the thoracic cavity, ensuring they are kept to a water seal for drainage management.
